_comment_1: ATOMIC GRANULARITY ONLY. One row per requirement, not per chapter. A 14-day project should produce on the order of 100 rows. If your row count is implausibly low for project age, the loop will self-terminate before the product is good. This is the Flatline failure mode.
_comment_2: Allowed status values: not_started | partial | done | out_of_scope. Every row should carry implementationRefs and testRefs once status >= partial. followupRefs link to F-NNN entries in FOLLOWUPS.html.

rows.requirement: A game is ten frames, played start to finish in the browser
Each frame gives the player three balls to clear ten pins
Strike (all ten on first ball) scores 10 plus the next two balls
Spare (all ten in first two balls) scores 10 plus the next one ball
Clearing all ten on the third ball scores a flat 10 with no bonus (candlepin-style), not a spare
Open frame scores one point per pin knocked down across the three balls
Tenth frame grants bonus balls (two for a strike, one for a spare, none for flat-ten/open)
Running cumulative score updates per ball and recomputes strike/spare bonuses retroactively
Between balls in a frame, count fallen pins and leave standing pins in place for the next ball
At frame end, perform a full re-rack of all ten pins
After the tenth frame the game ends and shows a final score and summary
Scoreboard shows all ten frames, per-ball counts, and frame marks (strike, spare, flat ten, open)
Each pin is tethered at the neck by a cord to a fixed overhead anchor above its home spot
Cords carry enough slack that pins fall, slide, and collide freely on ball impact
The tether is a length-limited rope/distance joint constraint in the physics engine
A pin counts as standing only if upright within tolerance, at rest, and still on the deck footprint
A settle window runs before counting so wobbling pins are not miscounted
Reset cycle runs settle, lift fallen, reposition, lower and ready in roughly three to five seconds
Between balls the whole rack is reeled up (recall-all) when pins were knocked down, then standing pins are re-spotted and fallen pins stay aloft; on a full re-rack all ten are lifted and set home
No sweep mechanism: fallen pins are lifted out by their cords, never wiped off the deck
Between balls the standing pins are re-spotted onto their home spots (a nudged-but-standing pin returns to its home spot) while fallen pins stay reeled up and aloft
A pin left hanging by its cord counts as fallen per the detection rule
A taut cord can yank a neighboring pin, emergent from the constraint sim, not scripted

Crossed/tangled cords are tolerated visually without the simulation deadlocking; next re-rack clears geometry. The reset lift is genuinely cord-tension driven (pins reeled up by the neck, hanging and swinging under gravity, never snapped upright). Tangles are RARE and GENUINE: only when a downed pin lies across another pin's cord so a pin cannot reel to its clearance height. A clean rack reels straight up with no shake; only a genuine snag runs a bounded up/down motor shake recovery, force-clearing at the retry cap.
Lane geometry and all tunable sizes/masses live in a single LANE config constant
Duckpin pins use a squat, belly-heavy collider and mass tuned for low energy transfer
rows.note: The squat belly-heavy collider/mass were already in place. The visual mesh now matches: PinSet builds a THREE.LatheGeometry revolved from a pure duckpinProfilePoints silhouette (small foot, fat low belly, pinched neck, small crown) instead of a plain cylinder, so the pins read clearly as duckpins. Collider stays a belly-radius cylinder and the tuned physics (mass, scatter REQ-030, cord neck anchor REQ-013/014, detection) is unchanged. Painted-wood material detail and the red neck band (look-and-feel) remain (F-015), so this stays partial.

rows.requirement: Ten pins are arranged in the standard triangle with correct spacing
Ball is a palm-sized sphere with no finger holes and duckpin-appropriate mass
Ball rolls down the lane under physics from the launch impulse
Pin-to-pin collisions chain with low scatter, tuned so real strikes stay genuinely rare
rows.note: Beyond the squat belly-heavy mass (REQ-026), the pin contact material is now a deliberate, single-source PIN_PHYSICS config block (friction 0.55, restitution 0). Zero restitution means struck pins barely rebound, so the collision chain dies out fast instead of spraying the rack like tenpin; a small playtest sweep showed any non-zero restitution roughly doubling the furthest pin's travel. Applied to every pin collider in src/pins.ts. A real-Rapier scatter smoke (tests/scatter.smoke.test.ts) throws straight balls across a sweep of lateral aim points and speeds and proves the duckpin feel: a dead-centre straight ball never strikes, no straight shot in the sweep strikes, most frames leave several pins, and no pin is flung sideways more than a few pin-spacings. Spin/angle remain the way to work for the rare strike (REQ-034/036).

rows.requirement: Gutters (smaller than tenpin) remove a ball that leaves the lane
Foul line is detected and an over-the-line release is handled
Step 1: player lines up the shot, setting lateral start position and base aim
rows.note: Lateral start position is settable via keyboard nudge and touch/mouse drag on an on-screen L/R indicator track (PR #18). Base-aim direction now ships: an off-centre stance points the ball back toward the pin deck via baseAimLateralSpeed (AIM config), threaded through ball.launch from the line-up alignment, so step 1 sets both where the ball starts and the direction it points. F-005 resolved. Playtest polish: the line-up stance now physically steps the first-person camera (shiftX translates only the eye in x while the look-at stays anchored on the centred down-lane aim point), so the pin deck visibly reframes from the bowler POV as the slider moves; the held ball still shifts with the stance and launch aim is unchanged (Q-022). Optional Ball Cam follow-cam polish: a persisted menu toggle (default off) makes the watching phase ride a damped chase cam behind the rolling ball (ChaseCam + chaseCamPose in src/camera.ts) instead of the fixed bowler view; aiming/spin/power and the settle-reset beat keep the normal pose, and the camera returns to the locked shooting view when the ball resolves (Q-023).

rows.requirement: Step 2: player stops a sweeping meter to set spin/angle (center is straight, sides curve)
Step 3: player stops a sweeping power meter to set ball speed and trigger release
Release combines line-up, spin, and power into launch velocity plus angular velocity; spin curves the roll
rows.note: ball.launch(spin, power, lateralOffset) now resolves all three inputs: the power stop sets down-lane speed, the spin stop adds a lateral nudge plus a vertical-axis yaw that hooks the roll, and the line-up stance adds the base-aim lateral velocity that points the ball back at the pins. ballLaunchVelocity composes spin and base aim additively over the power-resolved down-lane speed.

rows.requirement: Controls work via mouse, touch, and keyboard with a single confirm action per step
rows.note: All three steps confirm via one input on every device: pointer/touch tap and Space/Enter stop the meters and lock the line; keyboard arrows and now touch/mouse drag on the line-up track both set step-1 lateral aim (PR #18). Geometry maps are unit tested in tests/camera.test.ts.

rows.requirement: Spin and power meters render as mechanical gauges with a click on each stop, sweep speed/sweet-spot tunable
rows.note: The spin and power meters render as mechanical gauges on riveted metal plates (#meters in index.html): each has a recessed rail, a highlighted centred sweet-spot/straight band sized from the tuned config (SPIN.straightBand, POWER.sweetSpotBand) via the pure meterBandSpan in src/meter.ts, end labels, and a brass needle that slides to the live cursor each aiming frame using lineupMarkerOffset. Only the active step's gauge is shown. The click on each stop is the existing audio.playClick() cue (REQ-043). Sweep speed and sweet-spot width stay tunable in the SPIN/POWER config blocks. Playtest item 8: the verbose in-game status text was removed in favour of a Pins Mechanical style red/green throw light (#throw-light in index.html) plus the scoreboard. The pure throwLightFor(phase) in src/shotLoop.ts returns 'go' only in the aiming phase and 'wait' for every machine-owned phase; src/main.ts renderThrowLight drives the lamp data-light, the role=status aria-label ('Ready to throw' / 'Wait'), and a visible GO/WAIT plate word so the state is never colour-only (RULE 10). The only remaining HUD text is the first-run tutorial coach and a transient async-match submit prompt; the final score lives on the summary overlay. See gdd 08-controls #hud-throw-light.

rows.requirement: A single 3D lane scene is staged inside a machine-room interior
rows.note: The lane now sits inside an enclosed machine-room interior rather than floating in void. A new MACHINE_ROOM config block plus a pure machineRoomParts layout (src/config.ts) builds a dark shell (two side walls just outside the gutters, a back wall behind the pit, a front wall behind the bowler, and a ceiling above the pinsetter frame), and stages the background machinery the GDD #environment calls for: a conduit pipe run along each upper side wall (the new 'z' cylinder axis), a row of brass-rimmed gauge dials on the back wall, and the dim silhouette of a neighbouring lane rig off to one side. world3d.buildMachineRoom renders the shell with inward-facing BackSide material so the camera sees the interior, plus the conduit/gauge/neighbour dressing in the industrial palette (REQ-041). All set dressing, no colliders, staged from the pure layout well outside the playfield so it never touches the ball, pins, or cords. Verified headless (tests/machine-room.test.ts: the shell encloses the lane on every side, the ceiling clears the rig, walls rest on the floor, conduit runs down-lane high on each side outside the playfield, gauges sit symmetric on the back wall, the neighbour rig stands off to the side in the background) and confirmed in-browser (the room walls recede into the background and the neighbour-rig silhouette is visible, no console errors). Playtest item 13 (2026-05-30): added the metal ball return (Pins Mechanical style). A new BALL_RETURN block and pure ballReturnParts layout (src/config.ts) stage a polished-steel chrome rack cradle at the bowler-end wait spot plus a tilted two-rail return runway running down-lane, on the throwing-hand side just outboard of the approach (set dressing, no colliders, off the playfield), and a focused warm SpotLight over the pickup/return area so the returning ball reads well lit instead of dim. Bounds verified headless (tests/ball-return.test.ts).

rows.requirement: The pinsetter rig is visible on stage: guide tubes, cords, winding drums, overhead drive unit
rows.note: The string machine is now staged above the pin deck as set dressing. pinsetterRigParts in src/config.ts is pure layout derived from the rack home spots and a single tunable PINSETTER block: two longitudinal painted-red rails plus a cross beam over each pin row at the cord anchor height (PINSETTER.frameTopY = TETHER.topY, so the beams visibly carry the strings the pins already hang from), one blackened-steel guide tube hung below the frame over each pin (above the standing pins, below the frame), one winding drum per pin riding a per-row cross-shaft, and a dark cast-iron overhead drive unit mounted at the down-lane back end. world3d.buildPinsetterRig turns the parts into box and cylinder meshes via rigBeamMesh/rigCylinderMesh; the cords themselves were already rendered per pin in src/pins.ts (REQ-013). No colliders: the rig never touches physics or the cords' behaviour. The pure layout is unit tested headless (one tube and drum per pin, one shaft and cross beam per row, frame at anchor height, tubes clear of the standing pins, drive unit above and behind the rack, frame within lane width).

rows.requirement: Mechanical material palette (oiled wood, steel, brass) with warm industrial work-light lighting and fog
Scoreboard is styled as an electromechanical display (split-flap / rolling counters / relay digits)
rows.note: The scoreboard now reads as an electromechanical split-flap board on a blackened-steel plate. Every ball glyph and running total renders on a two-leaf split-flap card (top and bottom leaf split by a centre hinge seam) in src/scoreboard.ts, and when a cell's glyph changes between paints only that card gets a .vp-flip class and its top leaf folds over the hinge via the vp-flap-fold keyframe in index.html, the way a real split-flap board snaps a new digit. Which cells changed is a pure diff (cellGlyphs + changedCellKeys) so the flip targeting is unit tested without a DOM; the Scoreboard class adds/clears the class and is verified against a fake DOM. The flip is suppressed under prefers-reduced-motion (both in JS and the media query).

rows.requirement: Procedural Web Audio engine synthesizes pin clatter, ball roll, and the string-reset whir
Strike triggers a Rube Goldberg victory routine with mechanical-debris particles, screen shake, and a sound flourish
rows.note: A first-ball ten now runs a brief mechanical flourish alongside the existing audio strike sting (REQ-043). A new pure VictoryRoutine (src/victory.ts) spawns a burst of debris off the pin deck, integrates each bit under gravity and tumbles it, and produces a decaying camera-shake offset that oscillates on x/y (z stable) and zeroes after a short window. The VICTORY config block (src/config.ts) holds all tunables (debris count/spread/velocities, durations, shake amplitude/frequency, two-tone palette). The visual layer in src/world3d.ts is a pre-built emissive-spark/steel-scrap debris mesh pool with syncVictoryDebris to mirror the sim each frame; src/main.ts fires victory.start(LANE.headSpot) on a strike, applies the shake as an absolute offset from the captured resting camera pose so it rattles without drifting, and clears debris/shake when the burst ends. Durations sit inside the reset settle beat so the loop never drags. Motion is verified at the pure-sim level (tests/victory.test.ts) since jsdom cannot drive WebGL.

rows.requirement: A screens/state machine drives menu, in-game, and summary states
Settings (at least an audio enable toggle) persist across sessions
rows.note: Audio-enable was the first persisted setting; a persisted Ball Cam toggle (default off) now lives alongside it in the same localStorage blob, with its own menu button exposing aria-pressed.

rows.requirement: A minimal first-run tutorial communicates the three-step control scheme without a wall of text
A match is a shared ten-frame game with two-plus seats and server-authoritative state
rows.note: Done. The match model ships as a pure, JSON-serializable MatchState (src/match.ts): id, clamped 2-to-6 seat count (default 2, not hard-coded to two), per-seat name/secret/claimed/frames, status open/active/complete, and currentSeat/currentFrame, each seat bowling its own ten-frame line. State is authoritative server-side: api/match.ts persists the JSON document under the vibepins:match: keyspace and every mutation (join, submitTurn) goes through the pure server-side transitions in src/match.ts, never a client-claimed value. The full loop now ships end to end: frame-by-frame turn enforcement (REQ-049), per-ball submission re-scored by the shared scoreGame engine (REQ-053), completion locking and final standings (REQ-056), all driven from MatchClient (src/matchClient.ts) and verified in tests/match.test.ts (create/join, seat clamp, no-mutation transitions), tests/match.api.test.ts (create/join/resume/submit over the route, secret-in-header, public view never carries secrets), tests/match.smoke.test.ts, and tests/matchClient.test.ts.

rows.requirement: Turn order is frame-by-frame and the server enforces whose turn it is, rejecting out-of-turn submissions
rows.note: submitTurn (src/match.ts) is the single authority for turn order. A submission must come from the seat the per-seat secret owns, that seat must equal currentSeat, and the submitted frame must equal currentFrame, else it is rejected (403 not-your-turn / no-seat, 409 wrong-frame). Turn order is frame-by-frame: every seat bowls the current frame before currentFrame advances and currentSeat resets to 1. Exposed over PATCH /api/match (api/match.ts), secret via the X-Match-Secret header.

rows.requirement: Finishing a turn presents a shareable handoff link via native share / copy
rows.note: Done. The multiplayer hub overlay (index.html #match, wired in src/main.ts) presents the handoff link in the lobby and in the your-turn/waiting states after a frame, with both share affordances the GDD calls for. handoffLink (src/matchUI.ts) builds the absolute ?match=<id> URL (id only, never the per-seat secret). The lobby Copy Link button writes it to the clipboard with an observable label flash, degrading to a manual select when the clipboard API is unavailable. The play-mode Share Handoff Link button now uses the OS share sheet first: shareHandoff (src/main.ts) calls navigator.share with the handoffShareData payload (src/matchUI.ts) when the Web Share API is available (canShare-gated), degrading cleanly to the clipboard copy on platforms without it (F-009 shipped). Opening a ?match=<id> URL (matchIdFromSearch) jumps straight into the hub on the Join path. handoffShareData and handoffLink are unit tested in tests/matchUI.test.ts (link build/parse, share payload carries only the id-only link, never a secret).

rows.requirement: Clear your-turn vs waiting-for-player states, with the live scoreboard shown while waiting
rows.note: A dedicated match screen (src/screens.ts: menu -> match -> menu, plus match -> playing -> match for bowling a frame) hosts the hub overlay. matchViewMode (src/matchUI.ts) derives the one block shown: entry (create / join), lobby (seat roster + handoff link for seated devices), your-turn (unmistakable 'Bowl Frame N' button), waiting (calm 'Waiting for <name>' headline, not a spinner), and complete (final standings). The waiting and complete states render the live ranked scoreboard via renderMatchScoreboard (reusing the leaderboard board-row language), so a waiting player sees where everyone stands. A Refresh control re-resumes the held match. The your-turn state now bowls the frame in-browser: the Bowl button launches the live shot loop (reusing the solo Game/shot loop), each settled ball's pin-fall (dead balls included as zeros) accumulates via MatchFrameAccumulator (src/matchFrame.ts), and on frame completion matchClient.submitFrame posts the per-ball line and the shell returns to the hub showing the advanced state (waiting / your next turn / final standings).

rows.requirement: Lightweight identity: display name plus per-device token, with a per-seat secret authorizing submissions
rows.note: Done. Identity is a display name plus a per-seat secret minted with crypto.randomUUID on create/join and returned only to the claiming device (api/match.ts). The per-seat secret authorizes submissions: submitTurn rejects any device whose secret does not own the on-clock seat (REQ-049). seatForSecret resolves which seat a device owns for resume. The secret never appears in the public match view (toPublicMatch, asserted in tests/match.api.test.ts). The client binds the credential to the device per-match: Settings persists a per-match credential (seat, secret, name) keyed by match id (src/settings.ts, tests/settings.test.ts), MatchClient sends it via the X-Match-Secret header on resume and submit (src/matchClient.ts, tests/matchClient.test.ts), and the hub (src/main.ts) prefills and persists the display name (settings.playerName) so reopening a match on the same device resumes the same seat under the same name. casual-friends security per Q-007.

rows.requirement: Bowling client computes physics and submits per-ball pin-fall; server validates and computes the authoritative score
rows.note: Server authority shipped: a turn submission carries the frame's pin-fall (the balls array), and submitTurn (src/match.ts) re-validates the seat's whole line with the shared scoreGame engine (src/scoring.ts), rejecting incomplete frames, over-rack counts, and illegal ball values. The server, not the client, owns the scored line. MatchClient.submitFrame (src/matchClient.ts) PATCHes a completed frame's per-ball pin-fall with the per-seat secret in the X-Match-Secret header, never a claimed score, and replaces its held view with the authoritative public match. The in-game wiring now closes the loop: the your-turn state's Bowl button runs the same Rapier shot loop as solo (src/main.ts), and each settled ball's physics-derived pin-fall (standingBeforeBall minus standing-now, or zero for a foul / gutter dead ball) accumulates in throw order via MatchFrameAccumulator (src/matchFrame.ts), which mirrors the duckpin turn-end rules (strike / spare early-out, three-ball tenth). On frame completion the accumulated balls submit to the server, which re-scores; the client never sends a claimed score, only the per-ball pin-fall the sim produced.

rows.requirement: Matches persist server-side with a roughly one-week TTL so a day-long game never expires mid-play
rows.note: Done. Matches persist server-side in Upstash Redis as a JSON document under vibepins:match:<id> with a 7-day TTL (MATCH_TTL_SECONDS = 7 * 24 * 60 * 60), and the TTL is refreshed on every read (loadMatch sets expire on each GET) so an actively-played day-long game never ages out mid-play. tests/match.api.test.ts asserts a resume read refreshes the match TTL; tests/match.test.ts pins MATCH_TTL_SECONDS to one week.

rows.requirement: Create, join (claim a seat), and resume flows for a match
rows.note: Done. Create (POST, claims seat 1), join (POST with id, claims the next open seat, activates when full), and resume (GET ?id=, returns the public match plus mySeat for a valid per-seat secret) all ship in api/match.ts over the pure src/match.ts transitions. MatchClient.createMatch / joinMatch / resumeMatch drive these behind an injectable fetch port (src/matchClient.ts), persisting the returned per-seat credential through Settings (src/settings.ts) so a reopen resumes the same seat. All three are now wired into the shell: the menu Multiplayer button opens the hub overlay (index.html #match, src/main.ts), Create/Join run from the entry form (Join when a ?match= deep link was opened), a Refresh control re-resumes the held match, and opening a ?match=<id> link on boot jumps straight into the resume/join path. Verified in tests/match.api.test.ts (create/join/resume over the route), tests/matchClient.test.ts (client flows + credential persistence), tests/match.test.ts and tests/match.smoke.test.ts.

rows.requirement: On completion the match locks and shows final standings
rows.note: Locking shipped: once every seat has a full, scoreGame-complete ten-frame line, submitTurn flips status to complete and any further submission is rejected (matches the join guard). Final-standings ranking ships in computeStandings (src/match.ts): pure, re-scores every seat's recorded line with the shared scoreGame engine (never a claimed total), sorts highest-first with a deterministic seat-number tiebreak, and assigns standard-competition ranks (ties share a rank: 1, 1, 3); it does not mutate the match. renderStandingsRows (src/matchClient.ts) renders the ranked rows in the leaderboard board-row language, tags the rank-1 winner row, marks this device's own seat (data-you), and HTML-escapes player names. The on-screen completion overlay now shows the standings plate (index.html #match-complete, src/main.ts renderMatch): when a submitted frame completes the match the shell returns to the hub, matchViewMode resolves to 'complete', and the final-standings rows render in the overlay. Still deferred: posting a completed match line to the global leaderboard (REQ-058).

rows.requirement: A completed solo ten-frame game posts its authentic final score to the leaderboard
rows.note: On game over the summary screen shows a name field and a Submit Score button. The client (src/leaderboard.ts) turns the finished GameScore into the per-frame ball line via framesFromScore and POSTs it to api/leaderboard.ts, which re-scores it with the shared scoreGame engine; the server total is authoritative, never a client-claimed number. The player name persists in src/settings.ts and pre-fills the field. A double-submit guard locks the form after a successful post, and a failed submit is non-fatal (status line only).

rows.requirement: A completed line in an async match is eligible to post to the global leaderboard
rows.note: On match completion the shell posts this device's own seat line to the global board (src/main.ts postMatchLineToBoard, called from renderMatch's complete branch). Default: only this device's seat posts, under the name it claimed the seat with, so each line lands once with no cross-device duplicates and never under a guessed name (Q-resolved in OPEN_QUESTIONS). Leaderboard.submitFrames (src/leaderboard.ts) posts a raw completed number[][] line with source=match; it re-scores locally only to confirm completeness and never sends a claimed total, so the server stays the sole ranking authority (REQ-059). Double-posting is blocked by a persisted guard: Settings.markMatchPostedToBoard / hasPostedMatchToBoard / unmarkMatchPostedToBoard (src/settings.ts) record the match id before the round trip and clear it on failure so a later view can retry. The complete view re-renders on every refresh, so the persisted flag is what keeps a re-view from re-posting.

rows.requirement: Submitted scores are validated server-side against the duckpin rules; impossible scores are rejected
rows.note: Done. A submission carries the per-frame ball line, never a claimed total. Both servers re-score it with the shared pure scoreGame engine (src/scoring.ts) and reject anything impossible: api/leaderboard.ts rejects a non-array payload (400), an invalid line, an incomplete game, and an out-of-range score, persisting only the server-computed total; api/match.ts submitTurn rejects over-rack counts, illegal ball values, and incomplete frames (409) before recording the line. Verified in tests/leaderboard.test.ts (rejects an impossible line, an incomplete game, and a missing payload), tests/leaderboard.smoke.test.ts, and tests/match.api.test.ts / tests/match.test.ts (illegal-frame rejection).

rows.requirement: An all-time global top board (capped) is maintained
rows.note: Done. The all-time board is an Upstash sorted set under vibepins:leaderboard, ranked by final duckpin score descending and capped at MAX_ENTRIES = 100: each POST trims the board from the bottom (zremrangebyrank) once it exceeds the cap, so only the top 100 honest scores persist. GET serves the top-N slice (api/leaderboard.ts). The client reads it (Leaderboard.fetchBoard / fetchBoth, renderBoardRows in src/leaderboard.ts) and the all-time tab renders it in the standings overlay (src/main.ts, index.html). Verified in tests/leaderboard.test.ts (default GET returns the all-time shape; a POST past the cap trims with zremrangebyrank('vibepins:leaderboard', 0, total-cap-1)), tests/leaderboard.smoke.test.ts, and tests/leaderboard.client.test.ts (render rows).

rows.requirement: A daily board resets each day
rows.note: Done. The daily board is a per-UTC-day sorted set keyed vibepins:daily:<YYYY-MM-DD> from todayKey (api/leaderboard.ts). Because the key embeds the current UTC date, a new day writes and reads a fresh key (the board resets each day), and each write sets a 25-hour TTL so the previous day's key expires on its own. Each POST writes to both the all-time and the day key and trims the day to MAX_DAILY = 50. GET ?type=daily serves the day's slice; the client daily tab renders it (src/leaderboard.ts, src/main.ts, index.html). Verified in tests/leaderboard.test.ts (GET type=daily returns the daily shape; a POST writes under the current UTC-day key with the 25h expire) and tests/leaderboard.client.test.ts.

rows.requirement: The player's own best is shown in context (rank and nearby entries) even when off the top slice
rows.note: The standings overlay now surfaces the player's own standing when their best sits below the visible top slice (20 rows). The GET endpoint accepts an optional ?name; when present it reads the whole capped board and returns a context block via the pure rankContext helper (api/leaderboard.ts): the player's 1-based rank and score plus a small window (radius 2) of the entries above and below them, each carrying its own rank and an isPlayer flag. Name matching is case-insensitive on the sanitized name and the first match wins (the board is score-descending so that is the player's best). The client (src/leaderboard.ts) forwards the persisted player name on fetchBoard/fetchBoth, stores allTimeContext/dailyContext per board, and a pure renderContextRows builds a 'Your standing' window that renders only when the player is off the top slice (so it never duplicates a row already on screen). src/main.ts wires it: openBoard passes settings.playerName, renderBoard drops the context HTML into #board-context, and the player's own row is visually highlighted via data-you in index.html. Names are HTML-escaped at the render boundary.

rows.requirement: The leaderboard is reachable from the main menu and shown on the end-of-game summary
rows.note: The leaderboard is reachable from the main menu and the end-of-game summary via Leaderboard buttons that open a standings overlay (Back returns to the opening screen). The overlay shows an all-time/daily tab pair over a rolling-counter standings plate (split-flap-style rows of rank/name/score) in the electromechanical scoreboard language. src/leaderboard.ts gained fetchBoard/fetchBoth (GET /api/leaderboard?type&limit) and a pure renderBoardRows; src/main.ts wires open/close, the tab toggle (aria-pressed), fetch-on-open, and re-render. A failed load is non-fatal.

rows.requirement: A Vercel serverless plus Upstash Redis backend with zod-validated payloads serves both multiplayer and the leaderboard
rows.note: A Vercel serverless plus Upstash Redis backend serves both the leaderboard (api/leaderboard.ts) and async multiplayer matches (api/match.ts, vibepins:match: keyspace, full create/join/resume/submit/complete loop shipped). Request payloads are now zod-validated at the wire boundary (api/schemas.ts), matching the GDD text literally: the leaderboard POST submit (name, frames as a 1..10 array of 1..3 pinfalls in [0,10], source) and GET query (type/limit/name/mode), the match POST create/join bodies (name, seatCount in [MIN,MAX]) and PATCH submitTurn body (frame in [1,10], balls, id/secret). zod is the outer shape/bounds gate; the pure scoreGame engine stays the scoring AUTHORITY that ranks the board (REQ-059) and the pure src/match.ts transitions stay the turn-order/scoring authority (REQ-049, REQ-053). zod encodes (does not loosen) the existing clamps; a malformed, oversized, or out-of-range payload returns 400 before reaching the scorer or the store. Rejection paths are covered in tests/leaderboard.test.ts and tests/match.api.test.ts ('zod payload validation (REQ-064)'). Decision recorded in OPEN_QUESTIONS Q-017 (resolved, decision: add zod, superseding the earlier option-A note).
